description_long: This is a military-style single-propeller airplane with a camouflage green and gray color scheme, featuring distinctive black and white invasion stripes on the wings and fuselage. The wings are straight and low-mounted, each displaying a roundel insignia with blue, red, and yellow rings. The fuselage bears the code '3WOP' and a roundel, with a streamlined canopy positioned just behind the propeller. The tail section is conventional, with a single vertical stabilizer and horizontal stabilizers. The landing gear is visible and appears to be of the taildragger type, with two main wheels under the wings and a smaller tail wheel. The overall design is reminiscent of World War II-era fighter aircraft, likely inspired by the Supermarine Spitfire.
description: A World War II-era, single-propeller military airplane with green camouflage, invasion stripes, and roundel insignias, featuring a low-mounted straight wing and taildragger landing gear.
description_view_0: Side view showing the full length of the fuselage, propeller, cockpit canopy, tail, and wing roundel.
description_view_1: Front view highlighting the propeller, symmetrical wing span with roundels, and visible landing gear.
description_view_2: Opposite side view displaying the fuselage code, tail, and wing details, mirroring view 0.
description_view_3: Top-down view emphasizing the full wingspan, invasion stripes, and roundel placement on both wings.
attribute_wingspan_configuration: Low-wing, straight configuration with no visible winglets or pronounced dihedral/anhedral angles; wings feature roundel insignias and invasion stripes.
attribute_engine_type: Single nose-mounted piston engine with a two-blade propeller.
attribute_tail_design: Conventional tail with a single vertical stabilizer and two horizontal stabilizers.
attribute_landing_gear_type: Taildragger configuration with two main fixed wheels and a single tail wheel.
attribute_cockpit_placement: Forward-mounted, enclosed single-seat cockpit with a streamlined canopy just behind the propeller.
